    85→
    86→===== L02_LastIsMaximum.lean =====
    87→import Game.Metadata
    88→
    89→World "FinNavigation"
    90→Level 2
    91→
    92→Title "Last is the Maximum"
    93→
    94→Introduction "
    95→# Using Fin.val_last as a Rewrite
    96→
    97→`Fin.val_last` isn't just for computing concrete values — it's a
    98→**rewrite** tool. When you have `(Fin.last n).val` somewhere in
    99→your goal, `rw [Fin.val_last]` replaces it with `n`, often
   100→unlocking `omega` to finish the job.
   101→
   102→**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 5` has value at
   103→most `(Fin.last 4).val`.
   104→
   105→Strategy:
   106→1. `intro i` to name the element
   107→2. `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify the right side to `4`
   108→3. `omega` to close `i.val ≤ 4` (since `i.val < 5`)
   109→"
   110→
   111→/-- Every element of `Fin 5` has value at most `(Fin.last 4).val`. -/
   112→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 5, i.val ≤ (Fin.last 4).val := by
   113→  Hint "Start with `intro i` to name the element."
   114→  intro i
   115→  Hint "Now `rw [Fin.val_last]` converts `(Fin.last 4).val` to `4`."
   116→  rw [Fin.val_last]
   117→  Hint "The goal is `i.val ≤ 4`. Since `i : Fin 5`, you know
   118→  `i.val < 5`, so `i.val ≤ 4`. `omega` handles this."
   119→  omega
   120→
   121→Conclusion "
   122→The **rewrite-then-omega** pattern will be your main tool in this
   123→world:
   124→
   125→1. Use a val lemma (`Fin.val_last`, or the ones you'll learn next)
   126→   to simplify `Fin` expressions to plain natural numbers
   127→2. Use `omega` to close the resulting arithmetic goal
   128→
   129→Why is `Fin.last` the maximum? Because every `i : Fin (n+1)`
   130→satisfies `i.val < n+1`, which means `i.val ≤ n`. And
   131→`(Fin.last n).val = n`. So `i.val ≤ (Fin.last n).val`.
   132→
   133→In plain math: $\\forall\\, i \\in \\{0, \\ldots, n\\},\\; i \\leq n$.
   134→"
   135→
   136→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   137→DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos Fin.le_last
   138→
   139→===== L03_EmbeddingCastSucc.lean =====
   140→import Game.Metadata
   141→
   142→World "FinNavigation"
   143→Level 3
   144→
   145→Title "Embedding with castSucc"
   146→
   147→Introduction "
   148→# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type
   149→
   150→What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?
   151→
   152→**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
   153→returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.
   154→
   155→For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
   156→`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
   157→change — only the type's upper bound increases.
   158→
   159→The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
   160→$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$
   161→
   162→Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
   163→So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.
   164→
   165→**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
   166→"
   167→
   168→/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
   169→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
   170→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
   171→  intro i
   172→  exact Fin.val_castSucc i
   173→
   174→Conclusion "
   175→`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
   176→`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
   177→*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
   178→type now has room for one more element at the top.
   179→
   180→**Why not automatic coercion?** You might wonder why Lean doesn't
   181→just automatically treat `Fin n` elements as `Fin (n+1)` elements.
   182→The reason: Lean's type system doesn't have a built-in coercion
   183→from `Fin n` to `Fin m` for arbitrary `n <= m`. Each embedding must
   184→be explicit, and `castSucc` is the standard one for the `n -> n+1`
   185→step. This explicitness prevents silent type-widening bugs.
   186→
   187→(Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here —
   188→just as it did for `Fin.val_last` in Level 1. But
   189→`Fin.val_castSucc` is the tool you'll need when `n` is a
   190→variable, so practice using the named lemma.)
   191→
   192→The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
   193→(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
   194→- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
   195→- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)
   196→
   197→You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.
   198→
   199→In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
   200→\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
   201→"
   202→
   203→/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
   204→the same value but relaxing the upper bound.
   205→
   206→## Syntax
   207→```
   208→i.castSucc      -- dot notation
   209→Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
   210→```
   211→
   212→## Type
   213→`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`
   214→
   215→## Value
   216→`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)
   217→
   218→## When to use it
   219→When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
   220→`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
   221→-/
   222→DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"
   223→
   224→/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.
   225→
   226→## Syntax
   227→```
   228→rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
   229→exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
   230→```
   231→
   232→## When to use it
   233→When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
   234→-/
   235→TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"
   236→
   237→NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
   238→NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc
   239→
   240→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   241→DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos
   242→
   243→===== L04_MovingForwardSucc.lean =====
   244→import Game.Metadata
   245→
   246→World "FinNavigation"
   247→Level 4
   248→
   249→Title "Moving Forward: Fin.succ"
   250→
   251→Introduction "
   252→# Fin.succ: The Next Element
   253→
   254→There's a second way to go from `Fin n` to `Fin (n+1)`:
   255→**`Fin.succ`**, which **increments** the value by 1.
   256→
   257→If `i : Fin n` has value `k`, then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has
   258→value `k + 1`.
   259→
   260→Compare with `castSucc`:
   261→- `i.castSucc.val = i.val` (same value)
   262→- `i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (value plus one)
   263→
   264→Both send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, but they land at different
   265→positions. `castSucc` keeps you in place; `succ` moves you
   266→forward by one.
   267→
   268→The theorem **`Fin.val_succ`** states:
   269→$$\\texttt{i.succ.val} = \\texttt{i.val} + 1$$
   270→
   271→**Your task**: Prove this value relationship.
   272→"
   273→
   274→/-- `Fin.succ` increments the underlying value by 1. -/
   275→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1 := by
   276→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_succ i`."
   277→  intro i
   278→  exact Fin.val_succ i
   279→
   280→Conclusion "

   291→| `i.succ` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val + 1` |

   294→`Fin` navigation into plain arithmetic. The strategy for proving

===== L01_TheLastElement.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 1

Title "The Last Element"

Introduction "
# Fin.last: The Boundary Element

In the previous world, you learned that `Fin n` contains the numbers
$0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. But how do you name the *largest* element?

For `Fin (n+1)`, the largest element has value `n`. Lean gives it a
name: **`Fin.last n`**.

`Fin.last n` lives in `Fin (n+1)` — not `Fin n` — because `n` is
less than `n+1` but not less than `n`.

The theorem **`Fin.val_last`** extracts its value:
$$\\texttt{(Fin.last } n\\texttt{).val} = n$$

**Your task**: Show that `Fin.last 4` has value `4`.
"

/-- `Fin.last 4` has value 4. -/
Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by
  Hint "Use `exact Fin.val_last 4` to close this goal.
  (Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here.
  But `Fin.val_last` is the general tool you'll need when `n`
  is a variable.)"
  exact Fin.val_last 4

Conclusion "
`Fin.val_last n` tells you that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.

This is the analog of saying: the largest element of
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$ is $n$. In Lean, you need `Fin.val_last`
to access this fact because `Fin.last` is an opaque term —
the type system doesn't automatically compute its value.

In the next level, you'll use `Fin.val_last` as a **rewrite** tool
inside a larger proof.
"

/-- `Fin.last n` is the largest element of `Fin (n + 1)`.
Its underlying value is `n`.

## Construction
`Fin.last n : Fin (n + 1)`

## Example
`Fin.last 4 : Fin 5` — the element with value 4.

## Value
`(Fin.last n).val = n` (see `Fin.val_last`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.last as "Fin.last"

/-- `Fin.val_last n` states that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_last]    -- rewrites (Fin.last n).val to n
exact Fin.val_last n -- closes a goal (Fin.last n).val = n
```

## When to use it
When you need to compute or simplify the value of `Fin.last`.

## Example
```
-- Goal: (Fin.last 4).val = 4
exact Fin.val_last 4
```
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_last as "Fin.val_last" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.last
NewTheorem Fin.val_last

DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

===== L02_LastIsMaximum.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 2

Title "Last is the Maximum"

Introduction "
# Using Fin.val_last as a Rewrite

`Fin.val_last` isn't just for computing concrete values — it's a
**rewrite** tool. When you have `(Fin.last n).val` somewhere in
your goal, `rw [Fin.val_last]` replaces it with `n`, often
unlocking `omega` to finish the job.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 5` has value at
most `(Fin.last 4).val`.

Strategy:
1. `intro i` to name the element
2. `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify the right side to `4`
3. `omega` to close `i.val ≤ 4` (since `i.val < 5`)
"

/-- Every element of `Fin 5` has value at most `(Fin.last 4).val`. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 5, i.val ≤ (Fin.last 4).val := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i` to name the element."
  intro i
  Hint "Now `rw [Fin.val_last]` converts `(Fin.last 4).val` to `4`."
  rw [Fin.val_last]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val ≤ 4`. Since `i : Fin 5`, you know
  `i.val < 5`, so `i.val ≤ 4`. `omega` handles this."
  omega

Conclusion "
The **rewrite-then-omega** pattern will be your main tool in this
world:

1. Use a val lemma (`Fin.val_last`, or the ones you'll learn next)
   to simplify `Fin` expressions to plain natural numbers
2. Use `omega` to close the resulting arithmetic goal

Why is `Fin.last` the maximum? Because every `i : Fin (n+1)`
satisfies `i.val < n+1`, which means `i.val ≤ n`. And
`(Fin.last n).val = n`. So `i.val ≤ (Fin.last n).val`.

In plain math: $\\forall\\, i \\in \\{0, \\ldots, n\\},\\; i \\leq n$.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos Fin.le_last

===== L03_EmbeddingCastSucc.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 3

Title "Embedding with castSucc"

Introduction "
# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type

What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?

**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.

For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
change — only the type's upper bound increases.

The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$

Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.

**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
"

/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
  intro i
  exact Fin.val_castSucc i

Conclusion "
`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
type now has room for one more element at the top.

**Why not automatic coercion?** You might wonder why Lean doesn't
just automatically treat `Fin n` elements as `Fin (n+1)` elements.
The reason: Lean's type system doesn't have a built-in coercion
from `Fin n` to `Fin m` for arbitrary `n <= m`. Each embedding must
be explicit, and `castSucc` is the standard one for the `n -> n+1`
step. This explicitness prevents silent type-widening bugs.

(Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here —
just as it did for `Fin.val_last` in Level 1. But
`Fin.val_castSucc` is the tool you'll need when `n` is a
variable, so practice using the named lemma.)

The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)

You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.

In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
"

/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
the same value but relaxing the upper bound.

## Syntax
```
i.castSucc      -- dot notation
Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
```

## Type
`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`

## Value
`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)

## When to use it
When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"

/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

===== L04_MovingForwardSucc.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 4

Title "Moving Forward: Fin.succ"

Introduction "
# Fin.succ: The Next Element

There's a second way to go from `Fin n` to `Fin (n+1)`:
**`Fin.succ`**, which **increments** the value by 1.

If `i : Fin n` has value `k`, then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has
value `k + 1`.

Compare with `castSucc`:
- `i.castSucc.val = i.val` (same value)
- `i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (value plus one)

Both send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, but they land at different
positions. `castSucc` keeps you in place; `succ` moves you
forward by one.

The theorem **`Fin.val_succ`** states:
$$\\texttt{i.succ.val} = \\texttt{i.val} + 1$$

**Your task**: Prove this value relationship.
"

/-- `Fin.succ` increments the underlying value by 1. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_succ i`."
  intro i
  exact Fin.val_succ i

Conclusion "
(As with `Fin.val_last` and `Fin.val_castSucc`, `rfl` also works
here since the definition is transparent. But `Fin.val_succ` is
the rewrite tool you'll need when `n` is a variable.)

You now have the three navigation functions and their val lemmas:

| Function | Type | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.last n` | `Fin (n+1)` | `n` |
| `i.castSucc` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val` (unchanged) |
| `i.succ` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val + 1` |

The val lemmas (`val_last`, `val_castSucc`, `val_succ`) convert
`Fin` navigation into plain arithmetic. The strategy for proving
`Fin` facts is: **rewrite to values, then omega**.

In the next level, you'll combine these val lemmas to prove a
relationship between `castSucc` and `succ`.
"

/-- `Fin.succ` maps `Fin n` to `Fin (n + 1)` by adding 1 to the
underlying value.

## Syntax
```
i.succ      -- dot notation
Fin.succ i  -- explicit
```

## Type
`Fin.succ : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`

## Value
`i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (see `Fin.val_succ`)

## Comparison with castSucc
- `castSucc`: same value, bigger type
- `succ`: value + 1, bigger type
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.succ as "Fin.succ"

/-- `Fin.val_succ i` states that `i.succ.val = i.val + 1`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_succ]      -- rewrites i.succ.val to i.val + 1
exact Fin.val_succ i   -- closes the goal directly
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `Fin.succ` to the underlying value.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_succ as "Fin.val_succ" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.succ
NewTheorem Fin.val_succ

===== L05_CastSuccBelowSucc.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 5

Title "castSucc is Below succ"

Introduction "
# Combining Val Lemmas

You now know three val lemmas: `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_castSucc`,
and `Fin.val_succ`. Time to **combine** them.

If `i : Fin n`, then `i.castSucc` and `i.succ` both live in
`Fin (n+1)`. Which one is larger?

Since `castSucc` preserves the value and `succ` adds 1, clearly
`castSucc` lands *below* `succ`. Let's prove this.

**Strategy**: Rewrite both sides to values using their val lemmas,
then let `omega` close the arithmetic.

1. `intro i`
2. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` — converts to `i.val < i.val + 1`
3. `omega`
"

/-- `castSucc` always lands strictly below `succ`. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`."
  intro i
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` to convert both
  sides to plain natural numbers."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val < i.val + 1`. `omega` closes this."
  omega

Conclusion "
The **rewrite-to-values** strategy works for comparing any two
`Fin` expressions:
1. Apply val lemmas to simplify `Fin` navigation to ℕ arithmetic
2. Use `omega` to close the resulting goal

This is the standard recipe. It works because the ordering on
`Fin n` is defined through values: `a < b` in `Fin` means
`a.val < b.val` in `ℕ`.

The fact `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` means these two functions
never agree — they always land at different positions.
`castSucc` fills positions $0, \\ldots, n-1$; `succ` fills
positions $1, \\ldots, n$. They overlap on $1, \\ldots, n-1$
but at different *input* values.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos

400→import Game.Metadata
   401→
   402→World "FinNavigation"
   403→Level 6
   404→
   405→Title "castSucc and succ Never Agree"
   406→
   407→Introduction "
   408→# Different Embeddings, Different Outputs
   409→
   410→In Level 5, you proved that `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` — the
   411→value of `castSucc` is strictly less than the value of `succ`.
   412→The immediate consequence: **they can never be equal**.
   413→
   414→If `i.castSucc = i.succ`, then their values would be equal. But
   415→you just showed one is strictly less than the other. Contradiction.
   416→
   417→The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern you've been
   418→practicing:
   419→1. `intro i h` — assume `h : i.castSucc = i.succ`
   420→2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to values
   421→3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h` — simplify
   422→4. `omega` — `i.val = i.val + 1` is impossible
   423→
   424→This is a direct corollary of Level 5's strict inequality.
   425→"
   426→
   427→/-- `castSucc` and `succ` never produce the same output. -/
   428→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc ≠ i.succ := by
   429→  Hint "Start with `intro i h` to assume they're equal."
   430→  intro i h
   431→  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
   432→  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   433→  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h`."
   434→  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h
   435→  Hint "Now the hypothesis says `i.val = i.val + 1`. This is impossible
   436→  for natural numbers. `omega` closes the goal."
   437→  omega
   438→
   439→Conclusion "
   440→This completes the picture of how `castSucc` and `succ` relate:
   441→
   442→| Fact | Statement | Proved in |
   443→|---|---|---|
   444→| Value ordering | `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` | Level 5 |
   445→| Disjointness | `i.castSucc /= i.succ` | This level |
   446→
   447→The two embedding functions `castSucc` and `succ` have **disjoint
   448→images at each input** — for any given `i`, the element `castSucc`
   449→lands on is always different from where `succ` lands.
   450→
   451→This proof used a pattern that deserves a name: the **value
   452→reduction strategy for hypotheses**. When you have a hypothesis
   453→`h` about `Fin` elements:
   454→1. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — reduce to values
   455→2. `rw [val_lemma, ...] at h` — simplify to ℕ arithmetic
   456→3. `omega` — derive a contradiction or close the goal
   457→
   458→In Level 5, you used the goal-level version (rewrite the *goal*
   459→to values, then `omega`). Here you used the hypothesis-level
   460→version (rewrite the *hypothesis* to values, then `omega`). Both
   461→are instances of the same idea: **reduce Fin reasoning to ℕ
   462→arithmetic, then let omega handle it**.
   463→
   464→Combined with the separation facts (next levels) and the
   465→decompositions (later), this means `Fin (n+1)` is neatly
   466→partitioned by these two embedding functions.
   467→"
   468→
   469→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   470→DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos
   471→
   472→===== L07_CastSuccNeverLast.lean =====
   473→import Game.Metadata
   474→
   475→World "FinNavigation"
   476→Level 7
   477→
   478→Title "castSucc Never Reaches Last"
   479→
   480→Introduction "
   481→# The Key Structural Fact
   482→
   483→Here is the most important fact about `Fin` navigation:
   484→
   485→> `castSucc` and `Fin.last` produce **disjoint** elements.
   486→
   487→No element in the image of `castSucc` can equal `Fin.last n`.
   488→Why? Because `castSucc` preserves values in $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$,
   489→while `Fin.last n` has value $n$.
   490→
   491→To prove `j.castSucc ≠ Fin.last 3`, you need to show that
   492→assuming they are equal leads to a contradiction. The strategy:
   493→
   494→1. `intro j h` — assume `h : j.castSucc = Fin.last 3`
   495→2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to `j.castSucc.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
   496→3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h` — simplify to `j.val = 3`
   497→4. `omega` — contradicts `j.val < 3` (since `j : Fin 3`)
   498→
   499→This is the **value reasoning** pattern: convert `Fin` facts to
   500→value facts, then let arithmetic close the gap.
   501→"
   502→
   503→/-- No `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`. -/
   504→Statement : ∀ j : Fin 3, j.castSucc ≠ Fin.last 3 := by
   505→  Hint "Start with `intro j h` to assume equality for contradiction."
   506→  intro j h
   507→  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
   508→  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   509→  Hint "Simplify: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h`.
   510→  This gives `h : j.val = 3`."
   511→  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h
   512→  Hint "Now `{h}` says `j.val = 3`, but `j : Fin 3` means
   513→  `j.val < 3`. `omega` sees the contradiction."
   514→  omega
   515→
   516→Conclusion "
   517→This is the **separation fact**: the images of `castSucc`
   518→(values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) and `Fin.last` (value $n$) don't overlap.
   519→
   520→Together with the *maximum fact* from Level 2, this tells you:
   521→`Fin (n+1)` splits into two disjoint parts:
   522→- Elements with value $< n$ (the `castSucc` images)
   523→- The single element with value $= n$ (`Fin.last n`)
   524→
   525→This decomposition is like the natural number fact that every
   526→$k \\leq n$ either satisfies $k < n$ or $k = n$.
   527→
   528→The proof pattern — `ext_iff`, then val lemmas, then `omega` —
   529→is how you reason about `Fin` equality and inequality at the
   530→value level. You'll use it again in the boss.
   531→"
   532→

   545→# castSucc Preserves Distinctness
   546→

   548→`Fin.last`. But there's another important property: `castSucc` is
   549→**injective** — it never sends two different elements to the same

   551→

   554→(`i.castSucc.val = j.castSucc.val`), and since `castSucc` preserves
   555→values (`val_castSucc`), we get `i.val = j.val`, so `i = j`.

   558→boss) depends on `castSucc` being a genuine embedding — mapping

   560→two elements, the decomposition would be wrong.

   563→1. `intro i j h`

   565→3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h` — simplify
   566→4. `ext` — reduce the goal to values
   567→5. `exact h` — close with the hypothesis

   578→  This gives `{h}` as `i.val = j.val`."
   579→  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h
   580→  Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, then close with
   581→  `exact h`."
   582→  ext
   583→  exact h
   584→

   587→```
   588→rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   589→rw [val_lemma, val_lemma] at h
   590→ext
   591→exact h  -- or omega
   592→```
   593→
   594→Convert the hypothesis to values, simplify with val lemmas, then
   595→transfer to the goal. This is exactly the same pattern you'll use
   596→in Level 9 for `succ` injectivity — the only difference is which
   597→val lemma you use (`val_castSucc` vs `val_succ`).

   602→*perfect copy* of `Fin n` inside `Fin (n+1)`, preserving both
   603→values and distinctness.

   606→**injectivity**. In Lean, `Function.Injective f` means
   607→`∀ a b, f a = f b → a = b`. You'll meet this formal definition
   608→in the FinsetImage world, where injectivity controls whether

   624→# succ Preserves Distinctness

   651→  rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h

   663→| `succ` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` | `rw [val_succ, val_succ] at h` | `ext; omega` |
   664→
   665→The only difference: after simplifying `succ` values, `h` says
   666→`i.val + 1 = j.val + 1` instead of `i.val = j.val`, so you need

   676→===== L10_ZeroSuccDecomposition.lean =====
   677→import Game.Metadata
   678→
   679→World "FinNavigation"
   680→Level 10
   681→
   682→Title "The Other Decomposition"
   683→
   684→Introduction "
   685→# The 0/succ Decomposition
   686→
   687→In Levels 1-9, you studied the **castSucc/last** path:
   688→separation (Level 7), injectivity (Level 8), and the dual
   689→`succ` injectivity (Level 9). Now for the second decomposition.
   690→every element of `Fin (n+1)` is either `Fin.last n` or
   691→`j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n`.
   692→
   693→There is a **dual decomposition**: every element of `Fin (n+1)`
   694→is either `0` or `j.succ` for some `j : Fin n`.
   695→
   696→Why? An element `i : Fin (n+1)` has value `v` in
   697→$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$. If `v = 0`, then `i = 0`. If `v >= 1`,
   698→then `v = (v-1) + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where
   699→`j = ⟨v-1, ...⟩ : Fin n`.
   700→
   701→These two decompositions are **dual**:
   702→
   703→| Decomposition | Cases | Uses |
   704→|---|---|---|
   705→| castSucc/last | `i = j.castSucc` or `i = Fin.last n` | Appending (vecSnoc) |
   706→| 0/succ | `i = 0` or `i = j.succ` | Prepending (Fin.cons) |
   707→
   708→In the next world, you'll see how `Fin.cons` uses the 0/succ
   709→decomposition: the head goes to index `0`, and the tail goes
   710→to the `succ` indices.
   711→
   712→**Your task**: Prove the 0/succ decomposition for `Fin 3`.
   713→The strategy is the same case analysis pattern: destructure,
   714→split on the value, handle each case.
   715→"
   716→
   717→/-- Every element of Fin 3 is either 0 or a successor. -/
   718→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i = 0 ∨ ∃ j : Fin 2, i = j.succ := by
   719→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure:
   720→  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   721→  intro i
   722→  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
   723→  cases i with
   724→  | mk v hv =>
   725→    Hint "Now case-split on `v`: is the value `0` or `succ n`?"
   726→    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
   727→    cases v with
   728→    | zero =>
   729→      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
   730→      left
   731→      rfl
   732→    | succ n =>
   733→      Hint "Here `v = n + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where
   734→      `j = ⟨n, ...⟩ : Fin 2`. Choose `right`, provide the witness,
   735→      then prove the equality."
   736→      right
   737→      Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨n, by omega⟩`"
   738→      use ⟨n, by omega⟩
   739→      Hint (hidden := true) "Use `ext` to reduce to values,
   740→      then `rw [Fin.val_succ]`."
   741→      ext
   742→      rw [Fin.val_succ]
   743→
   744→Conclusion "
   745→You've proved the **0/succ decomposition**: every element of
   746→`Fin (n+1)` is either `0` (the minimum) or `j.succ` for some
   747→`j : Fin n` (a shifted element).
   748→
   749→Combined with the **castSucc/last decomposition** from Levels 1-8,
   750→you now have two ways to partition `Fin (n+1)`:
   751→
   752→| Decomposition | Singleton | Image |
   753→|---|---|---|
   754→| castSucc/last | `Fin.last n` (value $n$) | `j.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) |
   755→| 0/succ | `0` (value $0$) | `j.succ` (values $1, \\ldots, n$) |
   756→
   757→The duality is: castSucc/last splits off the *maximum*;
   758→0/succ splits off the *minimum*.
   759→
   760→This dual structure is the reason tuples can be built from either
   761→end: `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ`, and `vecSnoc` routes
   762→through `castSucc`/`last`.
   763→"

   768→===== L11_SuccNeverZero.lean =====
   769→import Game.Metadata
   770→
   771→World "FinNavigation"
   772→Level 11
   773→
   774→Title "succ Never Reaches Zero"
   775→
   776→Introduction "
   777→# The Dual Separation Fact
   778→
   779→In Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images never equal
   780→`Fin.last` — the **castSucc/last separation**.
   781→
   782→Here is the dual: `succ` images never equal `0` — the
   783→**0/succ separation**.
   784→
   785→Why? Because `i.succ.val = i.val + 1 >= 1`, but
   786→`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. They can't be equal.
   787→
   788→The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern:
   789→1. `intro i h` — assume `h : i.succ = 0`
   790→2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to values
   791→3. `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h` — simplify both sides
   792→4. `omega` — `i.val + 1 = 0` is impossible for natural numbers
   793→
   794→Notice the new val lemma: **`Fin.val_zero`** says
   795→`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. Just as `Fin.val_last` simplifies
   796→`(Fin.last n).val` to `n`, `Fin.val_zero` simplifies `(0).val`
   797→to `0`.
   798→"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 6

Title "castSucc and succ Never Agree"

Introduction "
# Different Embeddings, Different Outputs

In Level 5, you proved that `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` — the
value of `castSucc` is strictly less than the value of `succ`.
The immediate consequence: **they can never be equal**.

If `i.castSucc = i.succ`, then their values would be equal. But
you just showed one is strictly less than the other. Contradiction.

The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern you've been
practicing:
1. `intro i h` — assume `h : i.castSucc = i.succ`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to values
3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h` — simplify
4. `omega` — `i.val = i.val + 1` is impossible

This is a direct corollary of Level 5's strict inequality.
"

/-- `castSucc` and `succ` never produce the same output. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc ≠ i.succ := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i h` to assume they're equal."
  intro i h
  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h
  Hint "Now the hypothesis says `i.val = i.val + 1`. This is impossible
  for natural numbers. `omega` closes the goal."
  omega

Conclusion "
This completes the picture of how `castSucc` and `succ` relate:

| Fact | Statement | Proved in |
|---|---|---|
| Value ordering | `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` | Level 5 |
| Disjointness | `i.castSucc /= i.succ` | This level |

The two embedding functions `castSucc` and `succ` have **disjoint
images at each input** — for any given `i`, the element `castSucc`
lands on is always different from where `succ` lands.

This proof used a pattern that deserves a name: the **value
reduction strategy for hypotheses**. When you have a hypothesis
`h` about `Fin` elements:
1. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — reduce to values
2. `rw [val_lemma, ...] at h` — simplify to ℕ arithmetic
3. `omega` — derive a contradiction or close the goal

In Level 5, you used the goal-level version (rewrite the *goal*
to values, then `omega`). Here you used the hypothesis-level
version (rewrite the *hypothesis* to values, then `omega`). Both
are instances of the same idea: **reduce Fin reasoning to ℕ
arithmetic, then let omega handle it**.

Combined with the separation facts (next levels) and the
decompositions (later), this means `Fin (n+1)` is neatly
partitioned by these two embedding functions.
"

===== L07_CastSuccNeverLast.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 7

Title "castSucc Never Reaches Last"

Introduction "
# The Key Structural Fact

Here is the most important fact about `Fin` navigation:

> `castSucc` and `Fin.last` produce **disjoint** elements.

No element in the image of `castSucc` can equal `Fin.last n`.
Why? Because `castSucc` preserves values in $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$,
while `Fin.last n` has value $n$.

To prove `j.castSucc ≠ Fin.last 3`, you need to show that
assuming they are equal leads to a contradiction. The strategy:

1. `intro j h` — assume `h : j.castSucc = Fin.last 3`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to `j.castSucc.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h` — simplify to `j.val = 3`
4. `omega` — contradicts `j.val < 3` (since `j : Fin 3`)

This is the **value reasoning** pattern: convert `Fin` facts to
value facts, then let arithmetic close the gap.
"

/-- No `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`. -/
Statement : ∀ j : Fin 3, j.castSucc ≠ Fin.last 3 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro j h` to assume equality for contradiction."
  intro j h
  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h`.
  This gives `h : j.val = 3`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says `j.val = 3`, but `j : Fin 3` means
  `j.val < 3`. `omega` sees the contradiction."
  omega

Conclusion "
This is the **separation fact**: the images of `castSucc`
(values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) and `Fin.last` (value $n$) don't overlap.

Together with the *maximum fact* from Level 2, this tells you:
`Fin (n+1)` splits into two disjoint parts:
- Elements with value $< n$ (the `castSucc` images)
- The single element with value $= n$ (`Fin.last n`)

This decomposition is like the natural number fact that every
$k \\leq n$ either satisfies $k < n$ or $k = n$.

The proof pattern — `ext_iff`, then val lemmas, then `omega` —
is how you reason about `Fin` equality and inequality at the
value level. You'll use it again in the boss.
"

===== L08_CastSuccInjectivity.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 8

Title "castSucc is Injective"

Introduction "
# castSucc Preserves Distinctness

In Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images are disjoint from
`Fin.last`. But there's another important property: `castSucc` is
**injective** — it never sends two different elements to the same
place.

Why? Because `castSucc` preserves values. If
`i.castSucc = j.castSucc`, then their values are equal
(`i.castSucc.val = j.castSucc.val`), and since `castSucc` preserves
values (`val_castSucc`), we get `i.val = j.val`, so `i = j`.

This matters because the decomposition (which you'll prove in the
boss) depends on `castSucc` being a genuine embedding — mapping
`Fin n` *injectively* into `Fin (n+1)`. If `castSucc` could collapse
two elements, the decomposition would be wrong.

**Strategy**: The pattern is the same as succ injectivity:
1. `intro i j h`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert equality to values
3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h` — simplify
4. `ext` — reduce the goal to values
5. `exact h` — close with the hypothesis
"

/-- `castSucc` is injective: equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
Statement : ∀ i j : Fin 3, i.castSucc = j.castSucc → i = j := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i j h`."
  intro i j h
  Hint "Convert `{h}` to a value equality: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify the `castSucc` values:
  `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h`.
  This gives `{h}` as `i.val = j.val`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h
  Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, then close with
  `exact h`."
  ext
  exact h

Conclusion "
The **injectivity pattern** for `Fin` functions:
```
rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
rw [val_lemma, val_lemma] at h
ext
exact h  -- or omega
```

Convert the hypothesis to values, simplify with val lemmas, then
transfer to the goal. This is exactly the same pattern you'll use
in Level 9 for `succ` injectivity — the only difference is which
val lemma you use (`val_castSucc` vs `val_succ`).

Both `castSucc` and `succ` are injective — you've just proved it
for `castSucc`, and you'll prove it for `succ` next. Together with
the separation fact (Level 7), this tells you: `castSucc` is a
*perfect copy* of `Fin n` inside `Fin (n+1)`, preserving both
values and distinctness.

This property — **equal outputs imply equal inputs** — is called
**injectivity**. In Lean, `Function.Injective f` means
`∀ a b, f a = f b → a = b`. You'll meet this formal definition
in the FinsetImage world, where injectivity controls whether
image preserves cardinality.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 10

Title "The Other Decomposition"

Introduction "
# The 0/succ Decomposition

In Levels 1-9, you studied the **castSucc/last** path:
separation (Level 7), injectivity (Level 8), and the dual
`succ` injectivity (Level 9). Now for the second decomposition.
every element of `Fin (n+1)` is either `Fin.last n` or
`j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n`.

There is a **dual decomposition**: every element of `Fin (n+1)`
is either `0` or `j.succ` for some `j : Fin n`.

Why? An element `i : Fin (n+1)` has value `v` in
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$. If `v = 0`, then `i = 0`. If `v >= 1`,
then `v = (v-1) + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where
`j = ⟨v-1, ...⟩ : Fin n`.

These two decompositions are **dual**:

| Decomposition | Cases | Uses |
|---|---|---|
| castSucc/last | `i = j.castSucc` or `i = Fin.last n` | Appending (vecSnoc) |
| 0/succ | `i = 0` or `i = j.succ` | Prepending (Fin.cons) |

In the next world, you'll see how `Fin.cons` uses the 0/succ
decomposition: the head goes to index `0`, and the tail goes
to the `succ` indices.

**Your task**: Prove the 0/succ decomposition for `Fin 3`.
The strategy is the same case analysis pattern: destructure,
split on the value, handle each case.
"

/-- Every element of Fin 3 is either 0 or a successor. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, i = 0 ∨ ∃ j : Fin 2, i = j.succ := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure:
  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
  intro i
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
  cases i with
  | mk v hv =>
    Hint "Now case-split on `v`: is the value `0` or `succ n`?"
    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
      left
      rfl
    | succ n =>
      Hint "Here `v = n + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where
      `j = ⟨n, ...⟩ : Fin 2`. Choose `right`, provide the witness,
      then prove the equality."
      right
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨n, by omega⟩`"
      use ⟨n, by omega⟩
      Hint (hidden := true) "Use `ext` to reduce to values,
      then `rw [Fin.val_succ]`."
      ext
      rw [Fin.val_succ]

Conclusion "
You've proved the **0/succ decomposition**: every element of
`Fin (n+1)` is either `0` (the minimum) or `j.succ` for some
`j : Fin n` (a shifted element).

Combined with the **castSucc/last decomposition** from Levels 1-8,
you now have two ways to partition `Fin (n+1)`:

| Decomposition | Singleton | Image |
|---|---|---|
| castSucc/last | `Fin.last n` (value $n$) | `j.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) |
| 0/succ | `0` (value $0$) | `j.succ` (values $1, \\ldots, n$) |

The duality is: castSucc/last splits off the *maximum*;
0/succ splits off the *minimum*.

This dual structure is the reason tuples can be built from either
end: `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ`, and `vecSnoc` routes
through `castSucc`/`last`.
"

===== L11_SuccNeverZero.lean =====
import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 11

Title "succ Never Reaches Zero"

Introduction "
# The Dual Separation Fact

In Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images never equal
`Fin.last` — the **castSucc/last separation**.

Here is the dual: `succ` images never equal `0` — the
**0/succ separation**.

Why? Because `i.succ.val = i.val + 1 >= 1`, but
`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. They can't be equal.

The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern:
1. `intro i h` — assume `h : i.succ = 0`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` — convert to values
3. `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h` — simplify both sides
4. `omega` — `i.val + 1 = 0` is impossible for natural numbers

Notice the new val lemma: **`Fin.val_zero`** says
`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. Just as `Fin.val_last` simplifies
`(Fin.last n).val` to `n`, `Fin.val_zero` simplifies `(0).val`
to `0`.
"

#### W10: AbstractionLadder (Lecture)

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand the three-layer abstraction: `List α` (ordered, with duplicates) -> `Multiset α` (unordered, with duplicates) -> `Finset α` (unordered, no duplicates), and why each layer exists.

- Prove two lists are permutations

- L02: Lists are ordered: `[1, 2, 3] ≠ [3, 1, 2]`. Lesson: order matters for equality.
- L03: `List.Perm` — `[1, 2, 3] ~ [3, 1, 2]`. Lesson: permutation ignores order.
- L04: `Multiset` — quotient of `List` by `Perm`. `{1, 2, 3} = {3, 1, 2}` as multisets. Lesson: order forgotten.
- L05: Multisets keep duplicates: `Multiset.count 2 {1, 2, 2, 3} = 2`. Lesson: multiplicity matters.
- L06: `List.Nodup` — a list with no duplicates. `[1, 2, 3].Nodup` but not `[1, 2, 2, 3].Nodup`. Lesson: the bridge to finsets.
- L07: `List.toFinset` / `Multiset.toFinset` — how to get from lists/multisets to finsets. Lesson: the quotient forgets duplicates.

#### W11: Fintype (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `Fintype α` as a typeclass asserting that `α` has finitely many elements, and will be able to use `Finset.univ`, `Fintype.card`, and the composition instances.

**World intro note**: The introduction text must include a clear warning that not every type is finite: `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance. Attempting `Fintype.card ℕ` is a type error. This is a core misconception — learners who have only worked with `Fin n` may assume finiteness is universal.

**Theorem families**:

- L01: `Fintype (Fin n)` — `Fin n` is finite. `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`. Lesson: the card theorem.

- L03: `Fintype Bool` — `Fintype.card Bool = 2`. Lesson: other finite types.
- L04: Products are finite — `card (Fin 2 × Fin 3) = 6`. Lesson: `card_prod`.
- L05: Sums are finite — `card (Fin 2 ⊕ Fin 3) = 5`. Lesson: `card_sum`.
- L06: Function types — `card (Fin 2 -> Fin 3) = 9`. Lesson: `card_fun` = m^n.
- L07: ℕ is NOT finite — `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance. Show that attempting `Fintype.card ℕ` fails. Lesson: finiteness is a property, not a given. Warning: not every type you know is finite.
- L08: `Fintype.card_congr` — if `α ≃ β` then `card α = card β`. Lesson: bijective counting. This is the first Equiv-based argument.
- L09 (Boss): Compute the cardinality of a composite type (e.g., `Fin 2 × Fin 3 -> Bool`) using multiple card_* lemmas.

**Misconceptions addressed**: `ℕ` has no `Fintype` instance — not every type is finite.

**Dependencies**: AbstractionLadder

**Pset partner**: PsetCardinality (W13)

---

#### W12: CountingTypes (Example)

**Type**: Example / Case-study

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will have computed cardinalities of several concrete finite types and connected abstract card_* theorems to concrete numbers.

   551→### Phase 4: Big Operators (Worlds 14-18)

   555→#### W14: BigOpIntro (Lecture)
   556→
   557→**Type**: Lecture
   558→
   559→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `∑ x ∈ s, f x` and `∏ x ∈ s, f x`, know the constructor lemmas for empty/singleton/insert, and be able to unfold sums over small concrete finsets.
   560→
   561→**Theorem families**:
   562→- `Finset.sum_empty`, `Finset.prod_empty`
   563→- `Finset.sum_singleton`, `Finset.prod_singleton`
   564→- `Finset.sum_insert`, `Finset.prod_insert`
   565→- `Finset.sum_congr`, `Finset.prod_congr`
   566→
   567→**Proof-move goals**:
   568→- Read and write `∑ x ∈ s, f x` notation
   569→- Unfold a sum/product over a small finset to individual terms
   570→- Use `sum_insert` to peel off one element
   571→- Use `sum_congr` to rewrite the summand
   572→
   573→**Inventory changes**:
   574→- `NewDefinition Finset.sum Finset.prod`
   575→- `NewTheorem Finset.sum_empty Finset.sum_singleton Finset.sum_insert Finset.sum_congr Finset.prod_empty Finset.prod_singleton Finset.prod_insert Finset.prod_congr`
   576→- `TheoremTab "BigOp"`
   577→- Disable: `norm_num` on early levels (force manual sum unfolding)
   578→
   579→**Level sketch**:
   580→- L01: Notation — what does `∑ x ∈ s, f x` mean? Read the goal state. Lesson: big-operator notation.
   581→- L02: `sum_empty` — `∑ x ∈ ∅, f x = 0`. Lesson: base case is 0 (not undefined!). Warning: empty sum = additive identity.
   582→- L03: `sum_singleton` — `∑ x ∈ {a}, f x = f a`. Lesson: sum over singleton.
   583→- L04: `sum_insert` — `a ∉ s -> ∑ x ∈ insert a s, f x = f a + ∑ x ∈ s, f x`. Lesson: peeling off one element.
   584→- L05: Unfold `∑ x ∈ {1, 2, 3}, f x` completely using repeated `sum_insert`. Lesson: concrete computation.
   585→- L06: `prod_empty`, `prod_singleton`, `prod_insert` — same story for products. Lesson: multiplicative analogues. Warning: empty product = 1.
   586→- L07: `sum_congr` — rewrite the summand pointwise. Lesson: rewriting under a binder.
   587→- L08: Connection to the abstraction ladder — `Finset.sum` is defined via `Multiset.sum`, which is defined via `List.sum`. Show that `∑ x ∈ s, f x` ultimately reduces through the Multiset layer. Lesson: retrieval of the List → Multiset → Finset pipeline from W10 in a new context. This is why the abstraction ladder matters — big operators sit on top of it.
   588→- L09 (Boss): Compute a sum over a specific finset by unfolding with `sum_insert`, rewriting with `sum_congr`, and simplifying. Integration of all constructor lemmas.
   589→
   590→**Misconceptions addressed**: Sum over empty set is 0, not undefined; product over empty set is 1.
   591→
   592→**Dependencies**: FinsetOperations (needs membership, insert)
   593→
   594→**Pset partner**: PsetBigOp (W18)

   598→#### W15: BigOpAlgebra (Lecture)
   599→
   600→**Type**: Lecture
   601→
   602→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will be able to manipulate big operators algebraically: splitting sums, filtering, constant sums, and distributivity.
   603→
   604→**Theorem families**:
   605→- `Finset.sum_union` (disjoint case)
   606→- `Finset.sum_filter`
   607→- `Finset.sum_const`
   608→- `Finset.sum_add_distrib`
   609→- `Finset.prod_mul_distrib`
   610→- `Finset.sum_comm` (double sum interchange)
   611→
   612→**Proof-move goals**:
   613→- Split a sum by disjoint union
   614→- Filter a sum by a predicate
   615→- Simplify constant sums: `∑ x ∈ s, c = card s * c`
   616→- Distribute addition inside sums / multiplication inside products
   617→- Interchange order of double summation
   618→
   619→**Inventory changes**:
   620→- `NewTactic calc ring ring_nf` — introduce calc blocks for chained equalities; `ring`/`ring_nf` for algebraic simplification in sum manipulation
   621→- `NewTheorem Finset.sum_union Finset.sum_filter Finset.sum_const Finset.sum_add_distrib Finset.prod_mul_distrib Finset.sum_comm`
   622→
   623→**Level sketch**:
   624→- L01: `sum_union` — split a sum over a disjoint union. Lesson: decomposition by partition.
   625→- L02: `sum_filter` — filter the summation set. Lesson: conditional sums.
   626→- L03: `sum_const` — `∑ x ∈ s, c = card s * c`. Lesson: constant sums.
   627→- L04: `sum_add_distrib` — `∑ x ∈ s, (f x + g x) = ∑ x ∈ s, f x + ∑ x ∈ s, g x`. Lesson: linearity of sums.
   628→- L05: `prod_mul_distrib` — multiplicative analogue. Lesson: products distribute.
   629→- L06: `sum_comm` — interchange `∑ x, ∑ y, f x y = ∑ y, ∑ x, f x y`. Lesson: double sums.
   630→- L07: Rewriting under binders with `sum_congr rfl (fun x hx => ...)`. Lesson: targeted rewriting inside ∑.
   631→- L08: `calc` blocks for multi-step sum manipulation. Chain equalities: `∑ x ∈ s, f x = ... = ... = result` using `calc`. Lesson: `calc` blocks organize multi-step rewrites into readable chains. This is a core Lean skill for algebraic proofs.
   632→- L09: `ring` and `ring_nf` — use `ring` to close algebraic goals and `ring_nf` to normalize ring expressions during sum manipulation. Lesson: `ring` automates the algebra once you've reduced a sum to a pure arithmetic/algebraic expression. Warning: `ring` only works on ring goals (no hypotheses, no membership), so it complements rather than replaces the sum_* lemmas.
   633→- L10 (Boss): Prove an algebraic identity for sums requiring decomposition, distributivity, constant-sum simplification, and `calc`/`ring`. Multiple algebraic moves integrated.
   634→
   635→**Dependencies**: BigOpIntro
   636→
   637→**Pset partner**: PsetBigOp (W18)

   641→#### W16: FinsetInduction (Lecture)
   642→
   643→**Type**: Lecture
   644→
   645→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will be able to prove big-operator identities by induction on finsets, using `Finset.induction_on` with base case `∅` and inductive step `insert a s` where `a ∉ s`.
   646→
   647→**Theorem families**:
   648→- `Finset.induction_on`
   649→- `Finset.cons_induction`
   650→- Pattern: base case (use `sum_empty`), inductive step (use `sum_insert`, rewrite, apply IH)
   651→
   652→**Proof-move goals**:
   653→- Set up finset induction (different from ℕ induction)
   654→- Handle the base case `∅` using `sum_empty`/`prod_empty`
   655→- Handle the inductive step: given `a ∉ s` and the IH for `s`, prove for `insert a s`
   656→- Use `sum_insert` in the inductive step
   657→- Apply the induction hypothesis and finish with arithmetic
   658→
   659→**Inventory changes**:
   660→- `NewTheorem Finset.induction_on`
   661→- `NewTactic induction` — extend to finset induction (same tactic, new context)
   662→
   663→**Level sketch**:
   664→- L01: Finset induction setup — see the induction hypothesis shape. Lesson: how finset induction differs from ℕ induction. The hypothesis `a ∉ s` is new.
   665→- L02: Base case practice. Prove a sum identity for `∅`. Lesson: base case is always `sum_empty`.
   666→- L03: Inductive step practice. Given IH, prove for `insert a s`. Lesson: use `sum_insert`, rewrite, apply IH.
   667→- L04: Full induction proof for a simple identity (e.g., `∑ x ∈ s, 1 = card s`). Lesson: the complete pattern.
   668→- L05: A slightly harder induction proof (e.g., `∑ x ∈ s, 2 * f x = 2 * ∑ x ∈ s, f x`). Lesson: more algebra in the inductive step.
   669→- L06: `cons_induction` — alternative form. Lesson: variant for when `Finset.cons` is preferred.
   670→- L07 (Boss): Prove a non-trivial sum/product identity by induction requiring `sum_insert`, IH application, and several algebraic rewrites.
   671→
   672→**Dependencies**: BigOpAlgebra
   673→
   674→**Pset partner**: PsetBigOp (W18)

   678→#### W17: SummationFormulas (Example + introduces factorial)
   679→
   680→**Type**: Example / Case-study (also introduces `Nat.factorial`)
   681→
   682→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will have proved several classical summation formulas using finset induction and big-operator algebra, and will understand `Nat.factorial` as a product.
   683→
   684→**Object**: Concrete summation identities over `Finset.range`, including the product-to-factorial connection.
   685→
   686→**Proof-move goals**:
   687→- Apply finset induction to `Finset.range`
   688→- Combine big-operator lemmas with arithmetic
   689→- See classical formulas formalized
   690→- Connect `∏ i in range n, (i + 1)` to `Nat.factorial n`
   691→
   692→**Inventory changes**:
   693→- `NewDefinition Nat.factorial`
   694→- `NewTheorem Nat.factorial_zero Nat.factorial_succ`
   695→
   696→**Level sketch**:
   697→- L01: `∑ i in range (n+1), 1 = n + 1`. Warm-up induction.
   698→- L02: `∑ i in range n, i = n * (n - 1) / 2` (or the equivalent without division). Classical sum formula.
   699→- L03: `∏ i in range n, (i + 1) = Nat.factorial n`. Product connects to factorial.

### Phase 4: Big Operators (Worlds 14-18)

---

#### W14: BigOpIntro (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `∑ x ∈ s, f x` and `∏ x ∈ s, f x`, know the constructor lemmas for empty/singleton/insert, and be able to unfold sums over small concrete finsets.

**Theorem families**:
- `Finset.sum_empty`, `Finset.prod_empty`
- `Finset.sum_singleton`, `Finset.prod_singleton`
- `Finset.sum_insert`, `Finset.prod_insert`
- `Finset.sum_congr`, `Finset.prod_congr`

**Proof-move goals**:
- Read and write `∑ x ∈ s, f x` notation
- Unfold a sum/product over a small finset to individual terms
- Use `sum_insert` to peel off one element
- Use `sum_congr` to rewrite the summand

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition Finset.sum Finset.prod`
- `NewTheorem Finset.sum_empty Finset.sum_singleton Finset.sum_insert Finset.sum_congr Finset.prod_empty Finset.prod_singleton Finset.prod_insert Finset.prod_congr`
- `TheoremTab "BigOp"`
- Disable: `norm_num` on early levels (force manual sum unfolding)

**Level sketch**:
- L01: Notation — what does `∑ x ∈ s, f x` mean? Read the goal state. Lesson: big-operator notation.
- L02: `sum_empty` — `∑ x ∈ ∅, f x = 0`. Lesson: base case is 0 (not undefined!). Warning: empty sum = additive identity.
- L03: `sum_singleton` — `∑ x ∈ {a}, f x = f a`. Lesson: sum over singleton.
- L04: `sum_insert` — `a ∉ s -> ∑ x ∈ insert a s, f x = f a + ∑ x ∈ s, f x`. Lesson: peeling off one element.
- L05: Unfold `∑ x ∈ {1, 2, 3}, f x` completely using repeated `sum_insert`. Lesson: concrete computation.
- L06: `prod_empty`, `prod_singleton`, `prod_insert` — same story for products. Lesson: multiplicative analogues. Warning: empty product = 1.
- L07: `sum_congr` — rewrite the summand pointwise. Lesson: rewriting under a binder.
- L08: Connection to the abstraction ladder — `Finset.sum` is defined via `Multiset.sum`, which is defined via `List.sum`. Show that `∑ x ∈ s, f x` ultimately reduces through the Multiset layer. Lesson: retrieval of the List → Multiset → Finset pipeline from W10 in a new context. This is why the abstraction ladder matters — big operators sit on top of it.
- L09 (Boss): Compute a sum over a specific finset by unfolding with `sum_insert`, rewriting with `sum_congr`, and simplifying. Integration of all constructor lemmas.

**Misconceptions addressed**: Sum over empty set is 0, not undefined; product over empty set is 1.

**Dependencies**: FinsetOperations (needs membership, insert)

**Pset partner**: PsetBigOp (W18)

---

#### W15: BigOpAlgebra (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will be able to manipulate big operators algebraically: splitting sums, filtering, constant sums, and distributivity.

**Theorem families**:
- `Finset.sum_union` (disjoint case)
- `Finset.sum_filter`
- `Finset.sum_const`
- `Finset.sum_add_distrib`
- `Finset.prod_mul_distrib`
- `Finset.sum_comm` (double sum interchange)

**Proof-move goals**:
- Split a sum by disjoint union
- Filter a sum by a predicate
- Simplify constant sums: `∑ x ∈ s, c = card s * c`
- Distribute addition inside sums / multiplication inside products
- Interchange order of double summation

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewTactic calc ring ring_nf` — introduce calc blocks for chained equalities; `ring`/`ring_nf` for algebraic simplification in sum manipulation
- `NewTheorem Finset.sum_union Finset.sum_filter Finset.sum_const Finset.sum_add_distrib Finset.prod_mul_distrib Finset.sum_comm`

**Level sketch**:
- L01: `sum_union` — split a sum over a disjoint union. Lesson: decomposition by partition.
- L02: `sum_filter` — filter the summation set. Lesson: conditional sums.
- L03: `sum_const` — `∑ x ∈ s, c = card s * c`. Lesson: constant sums.
- L04: `sum_add_distrib` — `∑ x ∈ s, (f x + g x) = ∑ x ∈ s, f x + ∑ x ∈ s, g x`. Lesson: linearity of sums.
- L05: `prod_mul_distrib` — multiplicative analogue. Lesson: products distribute.
- L06: `sum_comm` — interchange `∑ x, ∑ y, f x y = ∑ y, ∑ x, f x y`. Lesson: double sums.
- L07: Rewriting under binders with `sum_congr rfl (fun x hx => ...)`. Lesson: targeted rewriting inside ∑.
- L08: `calc` blocks for multi-step sum manipulation. Chain equalities: `∑ x ∈ s, f x = ... = ... = result` using `calc`. Lesson: `calc` blocks organize multi-step rewrites into readable chains. This is a core Lean skill for algebraic proofs.
- L09: `ring` and `ring_nf` — use `ring` to close algebraic goals and `ring_nf` to normalize ring expressions during sum manipulation. Lesson: `ring` automates the algebra once you've reduced a sum to a pure arithmetic/algebraic expression. Warning: `ring` only works on ring goals (no hypotheses, no membership), so it complements rather than replaces the sum_* lemmas.
- L10 (Boss): Prove an algebraic identity for sums requiring decomposition, distributivity, constant-sum simplification, and `calc`/`ring`. Multiple algebraic moves integrated.

**Dependencies**: BigOpIntro

**Pset partner**: PsetBigOp (W18)

---

#### W16: FinsetInduction (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will be able to prove big-operator identities by induction on finsets, using `Finset.induction_on` with base case `∅` and inductive step `insert a s` where `a ∉ s`.

**Theorem families**:
- `Finset.induction_on`
- `Finset.cons_induction`
- Pattern: base case (use `sum_empty`), inductive step (use `sum_insert`, rewrite, apply IH)

**Proof-move goals**:
- Set up finset induction (different from ℕ induction)
- Handle the base case `∅` using `sum_empty`/`prod_empty`
- Handle the inductive step: given `a ∉ s` and the IH for `s`, prove for `insert a s`
- Use `sum_insert` in the inductive step
- Apply the induction hypothesis and finish with arithmetic

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewTheorem Finset.induction_on`
- `NewTactic induction` — extend to finset induction (same tactic, new context)

**Level sketch**:
- L01: Finset induction setup — see the induction hypothesis shape. Lesson: how finset induction differs from ℕ induction. The hypothesis `a ∉ s` is new.
- L02: Base case practice. Prove a sum identity for `∅`. Lesson: base case is always `sum_empty`.
- L03: Inductive step practice. Given IH, prove for `insert a s`. Lesson: use `sum_insert`, rewrite, apply IH.
- L04: Full induction proof for a simple identity (e.g., `∑ x ∈ s, 1 = card s`). Lesson: the complete pattern.
- L05: A slightly harder induction proof (e.g., `∑ x ∈ s, 2 * f x = 2 * ∑ x ∈ s, f x`). Lesson: more algebra in the inductive step.
- L06: `cons_induction` — alternative form. Lesson: variant for when `Finset.cons` is preferred.
- L07 (Boss): Prove a non-trivial sum/product identity by induction requiring `sum_insert`, IH application, and several algebraic rewrites.

**Dependencies**: BigOpAlgebra

**Pset partner**: PsetBigOp (W18)

---

#### W17: SummationFormulas (Example + introduces factorial)

**Type**: Example / Case-study (also introduces `Nat.factorial`)

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will have proved several classical summation formulas using finset induction and big-operator algebra, and will understand `Nat.factorial` as a product.

**Object**: Concrete summation identities over `Finset.range`, including the product-to-factorial connection.

**Proof-move goals**:
- Apply finset induction to `Finset.range`
- Combine big-operator lemmas with arithmetic
- See classical formulas formalized
- Connect `∏ i in range n, (i + 1)` to `Nat.factorial n`

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition Nat.factorial`
- `NewTheorem Nat.factorial_zero Nat.factorial_succ`

**Level sketch**:
- L01: `∑ i in range (n+1), 1 = n + 1`. Warm-up induction.
- L02: `∑ i in range n, i = n * (n - 1) / 2` (or the equivalent without division). Classical sum formula.
- L03: `∏ i in range n, (i + 1) = Nat.factorial n`. Product connects to factorial.

   731→
   732→#### W19: BinomialCoefficients (Lecture)
   733→
   734→**Type**: Lecture
   735→
   736→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `Nat.choose`, Pascal's identity, symmetry, and the factorial formula. (`Nat.factorial` was introduced in W17.)
   737→
   738→**Theorem families**:
   739→- `Nat.choose_zero_right`, `Nat.choose_self`, `Nat.choose_one_right`
   740→- `Nat.choose_succ_succ` — Pascal's identity: `C(n+1, k+1) = C(n, k) + C(n, k+1)`
   741→- `Nat.choose_symm` — `C(n, k) = C(n, n-k)`
   742→- `Nat.choose_eq_factorial_div_factorial`
   743→
   744→**Proof-move goals**:
   745→- Compute `choose` for small values
   746→- Use Pascal's identity recursively
   747→- Apply symmetry to simplify choose expressions
   748→- Connect choose to the factorial formula (factorial already known from W17)
   749→
   750→**Inventory changes**:
   751→- `NewDefinition Nat.choose`
   752→- `NewTheorem Nat.choose_zero_right Nat.choose_self Nat.choose_succ_succ Nat.choose_symm Nat.choose_eq_factorial_div_factorial`
   753→- `TheoremTab "Choose"`
   754→
   755→**Level sketch**:
   756→- L01: Factorial review and `Nat.choose` introduction — `choose 4 0 = 1`, `choose 4 4 = 1`. Lesson: boundary values. (Factorial is known from W17; this level bridges to choose.)
   757→- L02: `Nat.choose n 1 = n`. Lesson: choosing 1 element.
   758→- L03: Pascal's identity: `choose (n+1) (k+1) = choose n k + choose n (k+1)`. Lesson: the recursive structure.
   759→- L04: Compute `choose 5 2 = 10` using Pascal's identity. Lesson: concrete computation.
   760→- L05: `choose_symm`: `choose n k = choose n (n - k)`. Lesson: symmetry.
   761→- L06: `choose n k = 0` when `k > n`. Lesson: boundary behavior. Warning: this is surprising.
   762→- L07: Factorial formula: `choose n k = n! / (k! * (n-k)!)`. Lesson: the closed form.
   763→- L08 (Boss): Prove a binomial coefficient identity using Pascal's identity and symmetry, requiring induction or recursive computation.
   764→
   765→**Misconceptions addressed**: `Nat.choose n k = 0` when `k > n`.
   766→
   767→**Dependencies**: FinsetInduction, Cardinality
   768→
   769→**Pset partner**: PsetCombinatorics (W23)
   770→
   771→---
   772→
   773→#### W20: Powerset (Lecture)
   774→
   775→**Type**: Lecture
   776→
   777→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `Finset.powerset` (all subsets) and `Finset.powersetCard k` (k-element subsets), and connect their cardinalities to `2^n` and `C(n,k)`.
   778→
   779→**Theorem families**:
   780→- `Finset.mem_powerset` — `t ∈ powerset s ↔ t ⊆ s`
   781→- `Finset.mem_powersetCard`
   782→- `Finset.card_powerset` — `card (powerset s) = 2 ^ card s`
   783→- `Finset.card_powersetCard` — connects to `Nat.choose`
   784→
   785→**Proof-move goals**:
   786→- Prove membership in powerset/powersetCard
   787→- Prove cardinality of powerset = 2^n
   788→- Connect powersetCard to Nat.choose
   789→- See the set-theoretic meaning of binomial coefficients
   790→
   791→**Inventory changes**:
   792→- `NewDefinition Finset.powerset Finset.powersetCard`
   793→- `NewTheorem Finset.mem_powerset Finset.mem_powersetCard Finset.card_powerset Finset.card_powersetCard`
   794→
   795→**Level sketch**:
   796→- L01: `Finset.powerset` — enumerate all subsets of `{1, 2}`. Lesson: what powerset produces.
   797→- L02: `mem_powerset` — `t ∈ powerset s ↔ t ⊆ s`. Lesson: membership characterization.
   798→- L03: `card_powerset` — `card (powerset s) = 2 ^ card s`. Lesson: 2^n subsets.
   799→- L04: `Finset.powersetCard k s` — the k-element subsets. Lesson: restricted powerset.
   800→- L05: `card_powersetCard` — connects to `Nat.choose`. Lesson: C(n,k) counts k-element subsets.
   801→- L06: Enumerate `powersetCard 2 {1,2,3}` — see the 3 two-element subsets concretely. Lesson: concretization.
   802→- L07 (Boss): Prove a result connecting powerset cardinality to binomial coefficients, requiring both set-theoretic and combinatorial reasoning.
   803→
   804→**Dependencies**: BinomialCoefficients
   805→
   806→**Pset partner**: PsetCombinatorics (W23)
   807→
   808→---
   809→
   810→#### W21: BinomialTheorem (Lecture)
   811→
   812→**Type**: Lecture
   813→
   814→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand and apply the binomial theorem (`add_pow`) and the row-sum identity (`sum_range_choose`).
   815→
   816→**Theorem families**:
   817→- `add_pow` — `(x + y)^n = ∑ k in range (n+1), choose n k * x^k * y^(n-k)`
   818→- `Nat.sum_range_choose` — `∑ k in range (n+1), choose n k = 2^n`
   819→- Connection between `add_pow` and `card_powerset`
   820→
   821→**Proof-move goals**:
   822→- Apply the binomial theorem to expand `(x+y)^n`
   823→- Specialize the binomial theorem to get counting identities
   824→- Prove `sum_range_choose` (possibly by specializing `add_pow` to x=y=1)
   825→- Use big-operator machinery in a combinatorial context
   826→
   827→**Inventory changes**:
   828→- `NewTheorem add_pow Nat.sum_range_choose`
   829→
   830→**Level sketch**:
   831→- L01: State the binomial theorem: `add_pow`. Lesson: what the theorem says.
   832→- L02: Specialize to `n = 2`: `(x+y)^2 = x^2 + 2*x*y + y^2`. Lesson: concrete case.
   833→- L03: Specialize to `n = 3`. Lesson: practice with larger expansion.
   834→- L04: Specialize to `x = 1, y = 1`: derive `sum_range_choose`. Lesson: row sum = 2^n.
   835→- L05: Specialize to `x = 1, y = -1`: derive alternating sum identity. Lesson: signs alternate.
   836→- L06: Connection: why `card (powerset s) = 2^n` follows from counting subsets of each size. Lesson: double counting preview.
   837→- L07 (Boss): Prove a binomial identity by applying `add_pow` with specific values and simplifying using big-operator algebra.
   838→
   839→**Dependencies**: Powerset, BigOpAlgebra

   841→**Pset partner**: PsetCombinatorics (W23)
   842→
   843→---
   844→
   845→#### W22: PascalsTriangle (Example)
   846→
   847→**Type**: Example / Case-study
   848→
   849→**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will have explored concrete properties of Pascal's triangle and used them to verify counting identities.
   850→
   851→**Object**: Pascal's triangle as organized by `Nat.choose` and `Finset.Nat.antidiagonal`.
   852→
   853→**Proof-move goals**:
   854→- Compute specific entries of Pascal's triangle
   855→- Verify identities on concrete values before proving them abstractly
   856→- Use `Finset.Nat.antidiagonal` to express pairs summing to `n`

   864→- L02: Verify `∑ k in range 6, choose 5 k = 32` concretely. Lesson: row sum.
   865→- L03: `Finset.Nat.antidiagonal n` — the pairs `(a, b)` with `a + b = n`. Lesson: new construction.
   866→- L04: Rewrite a sum over `range (n+1)` as a sum over `antidiagonal n`. Lesson: reindexing.
   867→- L05: Verify a specific case of `choose n k * choose k j = choose n j * choose (n-j) (k-j)`. Lesson: compound identity.
   868→- L06 (Boss): Prove a result about Pascal's triangle entries using multiple identities.
   869→
   870→**Dependencies**: BinomialTheorem
   871→
   872→**Pset partner**: PsetCombinatorics (W23)

### Phase 5: Combinatorics (Worlds 19-23)

---

#### W19: BinomialCoefficients (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `Nat.choose`, Pascal's identity, symmetry, and the factorial formula. (`Nat.factorial` was introduced in W17.)

**Theorem families**:
- `Nat.choose_zero_right`, `Nat.choose_self`, `Nat.choose_one_right`
- `Nat.choose_succ_succ` — Pascal's identity: `C(n+1, k+1) = C(n, k) + C(n, k+1)`
- `Nat.choose_symm` — `C(n, k) = C(n, n-k)`
- `Nat.choose_eq_factorial_div_factorial`

**Proof-move goals**:
- Compute `choose` for small values
- Use Pascal's identity recursively
- Apply symmetry to simplify choose expressions
- Connect choose to the factorial formula (factorial already known from W17)

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition Nat.choose`
- `NewTheorem Nat.choose_zero_right Nat.choose_self Nat.choose_succ_succ Nat.choose_symm Nat.choose_eq_factorial_div_factorial`
- `TheoremTab "Choose"`

**Level sketch**:
- L01: Factorial review and `Nat.choose` introduction — `choose 4 0 = 1`, `choose 4 4 = 1`. Lesson: boundary values. (Factorial is known from W17; this level bridges to choose.)
- L02: `Nat.choose n 1 = n`. Lesson: choosing 1 element.
- L03: Pascal's identity: `choose (n+1) (k+1) = choose n k + choose n (k+1)`. Lesson: the recursive structure.
- L04: Compute `choose 5 2 = 10` using Pascal's identity. Lesson: concrete computation.
- L05: `choose_symm`: `choose n k = choose n (n - k)`. Lesson: symmetry.
- L06: `choose n k = 0` when `k > n`. Lesson: boundary behavior. Warning: this is surprising.
- L07: Factorial formula: `choose n k = n! / (k! * (n-k)!)`. Lesson: the closed form.
- L08 (Boss): Prove a binomial coefficient identity using Pascal's identity and symmetry, requiring induction or recursive computation.

**Misconceptions addressed**: `Nat.choose n k = 0` when `k > n`.

**Dependencies**: FinsetInduction, Cardinality

**Pset partner**: PsetCombinatorics (W23)

#### W20: Powerset (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand `Finset.powerset` (all subsets) and `Finset.powersetCard k` (k-element subsets), and connect their cardinalities to `2^n` and `C(n,k)`.

- L06: Enumerate `powersetCard 2 {1,2,3}` — see the 3 two-element subsets concretely. Lesson: concretization.
- L07 (Boss): Prove a result connecting powerset cardinality to binomial coefficients, requiring both set-theoretic and combinatorial reasoning.

#### W21: BinomialTheorem (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand and apply the binomial theorem (`add_pow`) and the row-sum identity (`sum_range_choose`).

1→===== L01_FirstContactWithImage.lean =====
     2→import Game.Metadata
     3→
     4→World "FinsetImage"
     5→Level 1
     6→
     7→Title "First Contact with Image"
     8→
     9→Introduction "
    10→# Finset.image: Applying a Function to Every Element
    11→
    12→Given a function `f : α → β` and a finset `s : Finset α`, the **image**
    13→`s.image f` (or `Finset.image f s`) is the finset of all values `f x`
    14→for `x ∈ s`. This is the formal version of the set-theoretic image
    15→$f(S) = \\{f(x) \\mid x \\in S\\}$.
    16→
    17→The key membership characterization is `Finset.mem_image`:
    18→```
    19→Finset.mem_image : y ∈ s.image f ↔ ∃ x ∈ s, f x = y
    20→```
    21→
    22→To prove `y ∈ s.image f`, the proof recipe is:
    23→1. `rw [Finset.mem_image]` — converts membership to an existential
    24→2. `use x` — provide the witness (the preimage of `y`)
    25→3. Prove that `x ∈ s` and `f x = y`
    26→
    27→**Your task**: Prove that $3 \\in \\text{image}(n \\mapsto n+1,\\; \\{0,1,2,3\\})$.
    28→
    29→The preimage of $3$ under $n \\mapsto n+1$ is $2$, since $2+1=3$.
    30→And $2 \\in \\text{range}(4)$ since $2 < 4$.
    31→"
    32→
    33→/-- 3 is in the image of (n ↦ n + 1) applied to range 4. -/
    34→Statement : (3 : ℕ) ∈ (Finset.range 4).image (fun n => n + 1) := by
    35→  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_image]` to convert the membership goal
    36→  into an existential: you need to find some `x ∈ range 4` with
    37→  `x + 1 = 3`."
    38→  rw [Finset.mem_image]
    39→  Hint "The goal is `∃ x ∈ Finset.range 4, x + 1 = 3`.
    40→  Which `x` satisfies `x + 1 = 3`? Solve: `x = 2`.
    41→  Use `use 2` to provide the witness."
    42→  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use 2`."
    43→  use 2
    44→  Hint "After `use 2`, Lean automatically verified that `2 + 1 = 3`
    45→  (by computation). The remaining goal is `2 ∈ Finset.range 4`.
    46→  Rewrite with `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to get `2 < 4`, then `omega`."
    47→  rw [Finset.mem_range]
    48→  omega
    49→
    50→Conclusion "
    51→The image membership pattern:
    52→1. `rw [Finset.mem_image]` — reduces to an existential
    53→2. `use witness` — provide the preimage (Lean auto-verifies `f x = y`)
    54→3. Prove `x ∈ s` (the remaining goal)
    55→
    56→The challenge is step 2: choosing the right witness. You need to solve
    57→$f(x) = y$ for $x$ — here, $x + 1 = 3$ gives $x = 2$.
    58→
    59→In plain math: to show $y \\in f(S)$, exhibit $x \\in S$ with $f(x) = y$.
    60→"
    61→
    62→/-- `Finset.image f s` is the finset of all values `f x` for `x ∈ s`.
    63→
    64→## Notation
    65→`s.image f` or `Finset.image f s`
    66→
    67→## Key lemma
    68→`Finset.mem_image : y ∈ s.image f ↔ ∃ x ∈ s, f x = y`
    69→
    70→## Example
    71→`(Finset.range 3).image (fun n => n * 2)` gives the finset containing 0, 2, and 4.
    72→-/
    73→DefinitionDoc Finset.image as "Finset.image"
    74→
    75→/-- `Finset.mem_image` states that `y ∈ s.image f ↔ ∃ x ∈ s, f x = y`.
    76→
    77→## Syntax
    78→```
    79→rw [Finset.mem_image]       -- in the goal
    80→rw [Finset.mem_image] at h  -- in a hypothesis
    81→```
    82→
    83→## When to use it
    84→When the goal or a hypothesis involves `y ∈ s.image f`.
    85→After rewriting, the goal becomes an existential that you solve
    86→with `use`.
    87→
    88→## Example
    89→```
    90→-- Goal: 6 ∈ (Finset.range 5).image (fun n => n * 2)
    91→rw [Finset.mem_image]
    92→-- Goal: ∃ x ∈ Finset.range 5, x * 2 = 6
    93→use 3
    94→```
    95→-/
    96→TheoremDoc Finset.mem_image as "Finset.mem_image" in "Finset"
    97→
    98→TheoremTab "Finset"
    99→NewDefinition Finset.image
   100→NewTheorem Finset.mem_image
   101→
   102→DisabledTactic trivial «decide» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto push_neg linarith
   103→DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_image_of_mem Finset.image_subset_image Finset.image_mono
   104→
   105→===== L02_ChoosingTheWitness.lean =====
   106→import Game.Metadata
   107→
   108→World "FinsetImage"
   109→Level 2
   110→
   111→Title "Choosing the Right Witness"
   112→
   113→Introduction "
   114→# A Different Kind of Finset
   115→
   116→In Level 1, the finset was `Finset.range n` and membership used
   117→`Finset.mem_range`. But finsets can also be built from explicit
   118→elements using `{a, b, c}` notation (which desugars to `insert`
   119→and `singleton`).
   120→
   121→**Your task**: Prove that $25 \\in \\text{image}(n \\mapsto n^2,\\; \\{2, 5, 8\\})$.
   122→
   123→The preimage of $25$ under $n \\mapsto n^2$ is $5$, since $5^2 = 25$.
   124→And $5 \\in \\{2, 5, 8\\}$ because it's an explicit member.
   125→
   126→The proof starts the same (`rw [Finset.mem_image]; use 5`), but the
   127→membership subgoal uses `Finset.mem_insert` and `Finset.mem_singleton`
   128→instead of `Finset.mem_range`.
   129→"
   130→
   131→/-- 25 is in the image of (n ↦ n ^ 2) applied to {2, 5, 8}. -/
   132→Statement : (25 : ℕ) ∈ ({2, 5, 8} : Finset ℕ).image (fun n => n ^ 2) := by
   133→  Hint "Same first step: `rw [Finset.mem_image]` to convert to an existential."
   134→  rw [Finset.mem_image]
   135→  Hint "Solve: $n^2 = 25$ gives $n = 5$. Try `use 5`."
   136→  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use 5`."
   137→  use 5
   138→  Hint "After `use 5`, two subgoals remain:
   139→  1. `5 ∈ the finset` (membership)
   140→  2. `5 ^ 2 = 25` (the functional equation)
   141→
   142→  Use `constructor` to split them."
   143→  constructor
   144→  · Hint "The goal is membership in an insert-based finset.
   145→    For these finsets, the membership lemma is:

   151→    rw [Finset.mem_insert]

   154→    right

   156→    Rewrite again: `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`."
   157→    rw [Finset.mem_insert]

   159→    left
   160→    rfl

   162→    rfl
